column50:
I believe one important point, which in general eats up a lot of time, in almost every task is the research part - getting to the right information, the right process description, understanding to whom it relates to - when I have more complex cases at hand (for example a legal entity change, insolvency cases, a mews-to-mews re-onboarding), it isn't clear what I have to do. Even with Glean there is a lot of information there, where I am not sure if it relates to what I have to do or what someone else will be doing. 
I believe a general clean-up of the processes is also needed, which would reduce the time we invest in. these tasks (added to more automation)

column50: The survey asks for "Average time per occurrence", which can be e.g. 1h for a task. And it asks how regularly this happens. It doesn't ask for how many of those tasks of a specific type we have to work on. For example if I have to work on billing / invoicing admin tasks weekly, but per week it's 20 of those tasks. I think it doesn't capture the volume of tasks to really reflect where we currently see the biggest burden / pain points.

column9: The data we use today is spread across multiple tools (Power BI, Looker, Secoda), so collecting data points, benchmarks, and research manually takes a lot of time. That’s why Secoda is such a game changer. I recently prepared an entire business review using only Secoda, and it was incredibly easy while also enabling much richer conversations. I’m very happy to share that example if you’re interested in seeing how I used it. (Prep "2h", Execution "1h", Follow-Up "2h")

column41: By simplifying and streamlining our processes. A good example is in my opinion the contracting process, it is so complex designed - do we even need this level of detail and complexity? Right now, I’m spending a lot of time on admin work, fixing issues, and chasing follow-ups.
column45: The internal meeting load is far too high, which takes away time we should be spending on client work. Many of these meetings also require preparation and follow-up, which adds even more distraction.
column49: The follow-up and escalation process is painful. Once we actually receive help, things move efficiently, but getting to that point is the biggest pain.
column50:
Because we’re involved in so many different topics and have countless tiny, hyper-complex processes, we end up spending way too much time on admin every day. For me, it’s sometimes really up to 60% of my day, and that just can’t be the standard.

What I’d love to see is a real simplification and streamlining of our processes. For example, more self-service or marketplace-style functionality, where customers can make smaller adjustments themselves once they’ve signed a general framework agreement (like a GMA) with Mews. Of course, some processes can’t be fully changed due to compliance or legal reasons. But we shouldn’t have to manually touch and relearn every single detail every time – that’s just unnecessarily heavy and inefficient.

Shoutout to Elena Martin Bonke, who's been doing an exceptional job with the rollout of beds&bars.
column41: I feel strongly that data input should be fully automated with the additional AI tools we've been provided, we should aim to spend no time on data input and invest in more OKR related activities

column49: I believe we spend excessive amount on contracting activities due to the complexity of NEU and the many error messages related to the platform that requires input from either CRM, Deal Desk or FinOps to resolve for. Given their lead-time to respond to queries, this is adding additional lead time to get contracts out and delays revenue activation with re-contracting. I would strongly argue for a case of CS coordinator to be made available to CSM team, as we had during the trial phase. Alternatively, do look at automation using AI tools but the permission levels is a blocker.
column50: An aspect that would have a non-linear impact is making re-contracting as easy as ABC - this would be a critical win that allows us to view re-contracting as something very positive (which it should be).
